third-point-q1-portfolio.headline: Third Point exits Microsoft, slashes Nvidia in sweeping Q1 overhaul
third-point-q1-portfolio.body_md:
Daniel Loeb's Third Point LLC filed its Q1 2026 Form 13F on May 15, disclosing one of the most sweeping portfolio reshuffles the fund has made in recent quarters: a near-complete exit from Nvidia, a full sale of Microsoft, and the addition of Meta, Alphabet, and a cluster of semiconductor equipment makers.

The Nvidia position — which stood at roughly 2.95 million shares at the end of 2025 — was cut by 94%, leaving just 190,000 shares on the books at March 31. Microsoft was sold outright; the position had been valued at approximately $448 million. The reductions extended well beyond those two names. Rail holdings were gutted: Union Pacific went from 1.81 million shares to 100,000, and Norfolk Southern from 975,000 to 100,000. Third Point also exited stakes in PG&E, Chipotle, Constellation Energy, Alibaba, Spotify, and Thermo Fisher Scientific.

## Where the capital went

On the buy side, Loeb initiated new positions in Meta Platforms (90,000 shares), Alphabet (175,000 shares), and Broadcom (50,000 shares). More distinctive are three new semiconductor equipment holdings: KLA Corporation (11,000 shares), Lam Research (75,000 shares), and ASML (12,000 shares). The fund also added 869,563 shares of Hut 8, the crypto mining company, and took a position in aerospace supplier TransDigm.

The semiconductor equipment trio signals a specific thesis: the primary AI infrastructure trade has shifted from chip designers — companies like Nvidia that design the GPUs — toward the equipment makers that supply the tools used to manufacture them. KLA, Lam Research, and ASML sit upstream of the entire chip production chain. That framing — often called the picks-and-shovels argument — has circulated among institutional investors for at least a year, but the scale of the Nvidia reduction makes Third Point's adoption of it unusually explicit.

Loeb has not issued a public statement on the specific trades, and the investor letter for Q1 2026 remains behind closed doors. The 13F filing itself contains no explanatory text — it is a required disclosure of equity positions, nothing more.

## Following the herd, or setting the pace?

The broader Q1 2026 13F season, with filings due May 15, has shown a discernible pattern: Microsoft exits and Alphabet accumulation appeared across multiple institutional managers, suggesting Third Point's moves are not contrarian but broadly in line with where large-scale capital was moving during the first quarter. Berkshire Hathaway, in a separate filing, showed a portfolio that had contracted from approximately $274 billion to $263 billion across fewer positions — a different fund with a different strategy, but another data point in a season of notable repositioning.

Third Point's Q1 performance was slightly negative: the flagship Offshore Fund returned -0.6%, and the Ultra Fund -0.3%. The fund ended the quarter with approximately $2.08 billion in disclosed 13F equity holdings against total assets under management of roughly $12 billion — the rest held in instruments not captured by the quarterly filing.

What the filing cannot tell you is timing. Positions are reported as of the final day of the quarter; a fund could have built a Meta stake in January or March, or sold down Nvidia across the whole period or in a single week. Loeb's Q2 letter, expected in late July, may offer colour on the rationale — if it does, the semiconductor equipment thesis will either look prescient or premature by then.

trump-iran-nuclear-goal.headline: Trump says preventing Iran's nuclear programme would satisfy the U.S.
trump-iran-nuclear-goal.body_md:
On May 18, President Trump stated that if a deal can be reached preventing Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon, the United States would "probably be satisfied" — language that captures the longstanding U.S. red line but stops well short of explaining what terms, timeline, or verification arrangements Washington is prepared to accept.

The remark came as part of a larger announcement: Trump said he was calling off a planned military strike on Iran, scheduled for May 19, after the leaders of Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates asked him to hold off for two to three days. Their message, relayed via Truth Social post, was that talks were "very close" to producing something acceptable. Trump stood down — but instructed Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to be "prepared to go forward with a full, large scale assault of Iran, on a moment's notice" if an acceptable deal did not materialise.

## Familiar Words in an Unfamiliar Moment

The sentiment is not new. The White House published a compilation in March cataloguing 74 separate instances of Trump declaring that Iran "cannot have a nuclear weapon." What made the May 18 remark worth noting — if barely — was its conditional framing: *if* a deal prevents nuclear weapons, the U.S. will probably be satisfied. The "probably" and the open-ended condition together leave deliberately vague what else Washington would or would not require.

That vagueness matters. Negotiations brokered through Pakistan have stalled over several hard points: how long Iran must suspend uranium enrichment (Washington wants 20 years; Tehran offered five), what happens to Iran's existing enriched uranium stockpile (the U.S. wants 400 kilograms delivered out of the country), and how quickly sanctions relief would be phased in. Trump himself called the ceasefire "on massive life support" as recently as May 11, after rejecting Iran's latest counterproposal as "totally unacceptable."

## What the Statement Doesn't Say

Stripped of the surrounding announcement — the postponed strike, the Gulf-state intermediaries, the threatened "full assault" — the quote reads as a policy restatement with no operational content. It does not signal that talks have advanced, that any specific demand has been softened, or that a framework text is close. It confirms that U.S. red lines remain where they have been.

For oil markets, which pushed crude above $106 a barrel this week partly on Strait of Hormuz disruption fears, this quote alone provides no directional signal. The more significant data point embedded in the same announcement was the postponement itself — evidence that Gulf states are actively working to prevent another military escalation and that Trump is, at least for now, responsive to that pressure.

## What to Watch

The two-to-three day window cited by the Gulf leaders as sufficient for a near-deal puts the next inflection point on or around May 20–21. If a memorandum of understanding can be drafted through Pakistani intermediaries — covering an enrichment moratorium, a framework for sanctions relief, and Hormuz transit commitments — markets will likely react to it far more than to a presidential restatement of a principle declared at least 74 times before. Absent that text, the military standby order remains in force.

enhertu-fda-her2.headline: Enhertu approved in two new HER2-positive early breast cancer settings
enhertu-fda-her2.body_md:
The FDA on May 15 approved Enhertu in two new settings for HER2-positive early breast cancer—a regulatory milestone that extends the antibody-drug conjugate into a far larger patient population than the advanced disease it was first approved to treat.

The two new indications cover opposite ends of the surgical pathway. In the neoadjuvant setting—treatment administered before an operation—Enhertu can now be given followed by a standard regimen of taxane, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab (THP) for adults with Stage II or III HER2-positive disease. In the adjuvant setting—treatment after surgery—it is approved for patients left with residual invasive disease after having received standard neoadjuvant HER2-targeted therapy.

The efficacy data underpinning both labels are, on their own terms, substantial. In DESTINY-Breast11, the Phase III trial supporting the neoadjuvant label, Enhertu produced a pathologic complete response rate of 67.3%—no residual cancer detectable at surgery—against 56.3% for standard doxorubicin-cyclophosphamide–based chemotherapy followed by THP. The adjuvant label rested on DESTINY-Breast05, which showed a 53% reduction in invasive disease recurrence or death compared with Roche's trastuzumab emtansine (Kadcyla), the previous standard of care. Three-year invasive disease-free survival was 92.4% versus 83.7% for Kadcyla—a hazard ratio of 0.47, which is a large effect in a curative-intent setting.

The adjuvant decision arrived nearly two months ahead of the FDA's original July 7 deadline. Early approvals of that kind are uncommon and typically signal confidence in the submitted data package.

## Where the commercial story gets complicated

Regulatory approval and clinical uptake are different things. In the neoadjuvant space, Enhertu enters a pathway managed by well-entrenched combinations, and oncologists treating patients with curative intent weigh long-term toxicity profiles more carefully than in metastatic disease. Interstitial lung disease and pneumonitis—a known class risk for antibody-drug conjugates—occurred in 4.4% of DESTINY-Breast11 patients (including one fatal case) and in 10% of DESTINY-Breast05 patients. Those figures will inform prescribing behaviour.

The adjuvant label also carries a sequencing restriction: patients who received Enhertu in the neoadjuvant setting cannot then receive it again as adjuvant treatment. That limits the drug's footprint in sequential-care models and may complicate coverage decisions at payer level, though AstraZeneca and Daiichi Sankyo executives have indicated they expect physicians to re-treat patients where prior neoadjuvant exposure was limited.

Against Kadcyla in the adjuvant setting, the efficacy case is harder to argue away. Kadcyla had relied on this niche since losing the metastatic line to Enhertu years earlier; a hazard ratio of 0.47 puts that franchise directly at risk.

> "HER2-positive breast cancer is an aggressive disease, and our goal is to reduce the risk of recurrence as early as possible." — Dr. Shanu Modi, Memorial Sloan Kettering, principal investigator, DESTINY-Breast11

## What to watch

Enhertu is already tracking toward roughly $5 billion in annual sales. Q1 2026 combined revenues reached $1.4 billion, up 31% year-on-year. The two approvals triggered a $155 million milestone payment from AstraZeneca to Daiichi Sankyo—contractual recognition of the commercial stakes. Daiichi projects approximately $5.4 billion in Enhertu revenues for fiscal 2026.

Whether these new labels translate into material revenue acceleration depends on three things: formulary wins and payer coverage decisions at major cancer centres, guidance revisions when AstraZeneca next reports earnings, and the maturation of overall survival data—both trials' OS curves remain immature, and the long-term risk-benefit profile in a curative setting carries more weight than in late-stage disease. For traders tracking AZN: watch for guidance on early-breast penetration rates and listen for any commentary on Kadcyla erosion pace in the next Roche reporting cycle.

azn-fda-wins-valuation.headline: AstraZeneca's FDA wins raise questions on valuation, not just pipeline
azn-fda-wins-valuation.body_md:
On May 15, AstraZeneca and its partner Daiichi Sankyo secured two FDA approvals for Enhertu (fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan) in early-stage HER2-positive breast cancer — one for the neoadjuvant setting, one for adjuvant use — followed three days later by the FDA's green light for Baxfendy (baxdrostat), a first-in-class treatment for uncontrolled hypertension. Three approvals in the span of a week is an unusual run. Whether it justifies the company's current valuation is a separate, harder question.

## What the approvals actually are

The Enhertu neoadjuvant approval covers patients with Stage II or III HER2-positive breast cancer being treated before surgery. The recommended regimen is Enhertu for four cycles followed by the THP combination — taxane, trastuzumab (Herceptin), and pertuzumab (Perjeta). The data come from the DESTINY-Breast11 Phase III trial, which showed a pathologic complete response rate of 67.3% with the Enhertu-containing regimen versus 56.3% with the standard dose-dense anthracycline/taxane approach — an 11.2 percentage point improvement that reached statistical significance.

The adjuvant approval is for patients who have already gone through neoadjuvant therapy with a trastuzumab-based regimen and taxane and still have residual invasive disease in the breast or lymph nodes at surgery — the group that, until recently, would have received Roche's Kadcyla (trastuzumab emtansine, or T-DM1). The DESTINY-Breast05 trial, which enrolled 1,635 patients across North America, Europe, Asia, and Oceania, showed Enhertu cut the risk of invasive disease recurrence or death by 53% compared with Kadcyla. The three-year invasive disease-free survival rate was 92.4% for Enhertu versus 83.7% for T-DM1. The adjuvant approval arrived nearly two months ahead of schedule, with the original PDUFA date set for July 7.

There is a clinically important limitation in the label: patients who receive Enhertu as neoadjuvant treatment cannot continue it in the adjuvant setting under the approved prescribing information. This restriction narrows the addressable population and complicates sequencing decisions for oncologists — a wrinkle that contrasts with some of the earlier, broader descriptions of the drug's potential post-neoadjuvant role.

Baxfendy is a different kind of win. The drug is the first aldosterone synthase inhibitor to receive FDA approval anywhere, and the mechanism is genuinely novel: rather than blocking aldosterone at the receptor level, as spironolactone and eplerenone do, Baxfendy inhibits the enzyme (aldosterone synthase, also called CYP11B2) that produces aldosterone in the first place. In the Phase III BaxHTN trial — 796 patients already on multiple antihypertensives, including a diuretic — Baxfendy 2mg once daily produced a 15.7 mmHg reduction in seated systolic blood pressure from baseline, a placebo-adjusted reduction of 9.8 mmHg (p<0.001). AstraZeneca acquired the drug's developer, CinCor Pharma, in 2023 for $1.3 billion upfront — a 121% premium to CinCor's unaffected share price at the time.

## The valuation question, carefully framed

For investors watching AstraZeneca's pipeline execution, a week like this one is reassuring. The company's revenue in Q1 2026 came in at $15.29 billion, up 8% year-on-year and ahead of the $14.93 billion analyst consensus. Enhertu itself generated $1.4 billion in combined quarterly sales in Q1 2026, a 31% increase, with Daiichi Sankyo projecting global Enhertu sales of approximately $5.4 billion for its fiscal year ending March 2027. Jefferies has estimated Enhertu's peak sales potential at $10 to $12 billion across all indications.

But there are grounds for caution on the margin, particularly around whether the early-stage oncology approvals will move the needle as much as their headlines suggest.

HER2-positive breast cancer accounts for roughly 15 to 20 percent of all breast cancer diagnoses. The early-stage setting is more constrained than the metastatic one: a meaningful fraction of early-stage patients achieve pathologic complete response with existing regimens and are effectively cured, limiting the pool for post-neoadjuvant adjuvant therapy. For the adjuvant use, only patients with *residual* disease after neoadjuvant treatment qualify under the current label. That is a subset of a subset. The DESTINY-Breast05 data are compelling in relative risk-reduction terms — 53% is a large hazard ratio improvement — but the absolute numbers still trace a relatively narrow treatment population compared to, say, a first-line metastatic approval in an unselected or HER2-low population.

The competitive terrain is not empty. Roche's Kadcyla held the adjuvant setting for years, and while Enhertu's superiority data are hard to argue with clinically, Kadcyla's U.S. patent expires in September 2026. The arrival of biosimilar T-DM1 will complicate the pricing environment in the adjuvant space even as Enhertu moves in. Guideline incorporation, payer negotiations, and physician habit all carry weight in practice — and early-stage breast cancer treatment is typically delivered by community oncologists who are slower to adopt newer agents than academic centers.

The stock has slipped roughly 9% over the past month, trading at around £137 per share against an analyst consensus price target of approximately £165 — implying, on consensus figures, about 17% upside. Simply Wall St's fair value model suggests a larger discount, estimating the stock trades some 42% below intrinsic value. The trailing price-to-earnings ratio sits near 27.8, the forward near 18, broadly in line with large-cap pharma with demonstrated oncology and cardiovascular pipeline depth. The market, in other words, is not obviously expensive — but it is also not obviously cheap given the uncertainty about how quickly the new approvals convert to revenue.

Baxfendy is arguably the more interesting near-term catalyst. AstraZeneca CEO Pascal Soriot has publicly described it as "definitely a big product," with internal projections of $5 billion or more in annual peak sales in hypertension alone and up to $10 billion if the drug succeeds in additional studies — including a chronic kidney disease programme. Jefferies analyst Dennis Ding has noted that commercial execution, not clinical data, will be the differentiating factor now that Baxfendy is approved, where AstraZeneca's large cardiovascular sales force provides a structural advantage. Mineralys Therapeutics is advancing a competing aldosterone synthase inhibitor, but Baxfendy holds first-mover status.

## The broader picture

AstraZeneca has several simultaneous growth vectors in oncology that provide some insulation against the execution risks in any single segment. The company is building a broader breast cancer franchise that includes Datroway (a HER2-targeting ADC in development), camizestrant (an oral SERD targeting HR-positive, HER2-negative disease, with Phase III data showing a 56% progression-free survival improvement), and continued label expansion for Enhertu in HER2-low and HER2-ultralow disease, where the drug has already captured approximately 55 to 60% market share since its 2022 approval. Milestone payments of $155 million were triggered to Daiichi Sankyo on the back of the two Enhertu approvals this week, consistent with the commercial-sharing arrangements baked into the 2019 collaboration.

What to watch now: adoption data will matter more than the approval headlines. The neoadjuvant indication requires convincing oncologists to swap out an established anthracycline-containing regimen for an antibody-drug conjugate approach in Stage II and III patients — a significant change in practice with attendant safety monitoring requirements, including the boxed warning for interstitial lung disease and pneumonitis that appears on all Enhertu prescribing information. For the adjuvant indication, the transition away from Kadcyla toward Enhertu is more data-driven, but will face price competition as T-DM1 biosimilars enter the market.

The next meaningful data readouts to watch involve Enhertu's ongoing studies in HER2-low early breast cancer and the broader cardiorenal programme for Baxfendy. Quarterly sales figures from mid-2026 onward will offer the first real signal of how quickly physicians are incorporating the new indications into practice. At the current valuation, investors are essentially being asked to believe that the pipeline continues to execute — and that peak sales projections across Enhertu and Baxfendy are achievable, not just plausible. The approvals this week are evidence in favour of that view. They are not, in themselves, proof of it.

trump-rx-generics.headline: Trump expands discount drug scheme to cover 600-plus generics
trump-rx-generics.body_md:
The Trump administration moved on Monday to dramatically widen the reach of its TrumpRx drug pricing platform, adding more than 600 generic medications to a site that launched only three months ago with 43 branded drugs. The expansion — a near-sevenfold increase in the platform's formulary — came through partnerships with Amazon Pharmacy, GoodRx, and Mark Cuban's Cost Plus Drugs, whose founder stood alongside the president at the White House rollout.

The generics now listed include workhorse chronic-care drugs: atorvastatin for cholesterol, metformin for diabetes, lisinopril for blood pressure, clopidogrel as a blood thinner. These are not specialty pharmaceuticals with outsized margins. They are, by design, the commodity tier of the drug market — and their inclusion marks a shift in the initiative's character from targeted branded-drug discounts toward broad-population affordability.

## What TrumpRx actually is — and isn't

The platform does not sell drugs directly. It aggregates cash-pay prices from partner pharmacies, provides manufacturer coupons, and routes patients to the nearest low-cost option or home-delivery service. Critically, payments made through TrumpRx do not count toward insurance deductibles or out-of-pocket maximums — a meaningful limitation given that roughly 90% of Americans carry some form of drug coverage (KFF, February 2026).

Rena Conti, a health economics professor at Boston University, noted that insured patients typically fare better through existing coverage, while those without insurance or facing high deductibles stand to gain the most. The platform is also unavailable in California and Massachusetts, where state law restricts manufacturer coupons when a generic alternative exists.

Trump characterised the expansion in maximalist terms — "By incorporating this massive catalog of low-cost generics at TrumpRx.Gov, consumers will now have one source to ensure that they're getting the lowest possible cost on their prescriptions" — and separately called the platform "the greatest breakthrough in lowering health care costs in modern history." A YouGov survey conducted earlier this year found that support for drug pricing reform falls from 57% to 41% when the Trump brand is attached. The political incentives here are clear: the administration is staring down November midterms, active Medicaid cuts, and the expiration of enhanced ACA subsidies this year.

## The market read

For healthcare investors, the question is not whether TrumpRx saves patients money on metformin. It is who absorbs the margin pressure as low-price transparency becomes a sustained federal policy instrument.

GoodRx shares rose 2.9% on Monday — investors read the partnership as accretive to the company's aggregator model. For pharmacy chains, the picture is less comfortable. CVS reported that adjusted operating income fell 7.1% in the first quarter of 2026, attributing the decline partly to "pharmacy client price improvements" — the industry's standard locution for margin compression (CVS Q1 2026 earnings).

The expansion to generics is qualitatively different from the initial branded-drug push. Pharmacy benefit managers — the intermediary layer between manufacturers and payers — derive substantial spread on generics, particularly when reimbursement rates exceed what they pay pharmacies. A federal platform that routes patients to cost-plus pricing models, bypassing that layer entirely, is a structural challenge to PBM economics, not just a coupon programme.

Generic manufacturers themselves are not the primary target here: the sector already competes on thin margins, and Cost Plus Drugs' transparent 15%-over-cost model was not designed to squeeze them further. The pressure lands on the distribution and reimbursement infrastructure — PBMs, pharmacy chains, and benefit administrators — that has historically captured the arbitrage between list price and actual cost.

The White House has announced 17 pharmaceutical deals since September 2025, and TrumpRx has drawn more than 10 million website visits since its February launch. Whether visits are converting to actual purchases has not been disclosed. The second major expansion in under four months suggests the policy push is not winding down. Watch for whether accompanying PBM reform legislation advances in Congress, and whether the administration moves to extend its Most-Favored-Nation pricing framework — established by executive order in May 2025 — into the generic tier through statutory action before November.

harry-styles-london-spending.headline: Harry Styles fans set to spend £1bn on London summer shows
harry-styles-london-spending.body_md:
A dozen nights at Wembley Stadium this summer and, according to Barclays, Harry Styles will have coaxed more than £1 billion out of British wallets. The bank published a forecast Tuesday putting total fan expenditure — tickets, accommodation, transport, food, drink, merchandise — at £1.06 billion across the 12-night London residency, part of the "Together, Together" world tour running from June 12 to July 4.

The per-person figure is £981, spread across around one million ticket-holders. Break that down and it reads more like a short city break than a night at a stadium: average ticket price £143.20, accommodation £141.20, transport £103.10, food and drinks £103.10, and merchandise another £102. London being the only UK stop on the tour explains some of the premium — fans travelling from outside the capital have nowhere to split their costs across multiple dates.

> "Concert tourism is on the rise because of the extent to which people value unique, shared experiences." — Tom Corbett, managing director of sponsorship and client experience, Barclays

The numbers put Styles' fans ahead of both Taylor Swift's Eras Tour (£848 per person) and last year's Oasis Live '25 reunion (£766 per person) — a comparison Barclays was happy to volunteer. The bank has now issued near-identical reports for three consecutive blockbuster tours, establishing what has quietly become a PR formula: partner with Opinium, survey 2,000 consumers plus roughly 200 ticket-holders, extrapolate to total expected attendance, and attach a headline figure with nine zeros.

That methodology deserves a note of caution. Self-reported spending in surveys tends to run aspirational; people who've already bought tickets and agreed to participate in a music-fan survey are not a neutral sample. The 200 ticket-holders polled represent about 0.02% of the expected crowd. And Barclays — a major sponsor of live events — has an institutional interest in amplifying the economic story of live music. None of this makes the figure wrong, but it makes it a starting point rather than a verified number.

What the data does capture, fairly reliably, is the qualitative shift in consumer priorities. Sixty-four percent of those surveyed said they would have paid more for their ticket. Seventy-four percent described securing one as a status symbol. Twenty-seven percent called it a once-in-a-lifetime experience. These are the markers of inelastic, prestige-driven demand — the kind that holds up even as households trim other discretionary lines.

For UK hospitality and transport operators, that psychology is genuinely relevant. Twelve sold-out nights at an 80,000-capacity Wembley Stadium, concentrated between June and early July, will put real pressure on central London hotel inventory and Tube capacity around Olympic Way. Whether actual bookings and transaction data confirm the Barclays projection is the question worth watching. The bank's own monthly consumer spend report — drawn from card transactions rather than surveys — will provide a sharper read when summer figures land in late July.

Styles also sets a new record with this run: more solo-artist performances at Wembley in a single calendar year than anyone before him, breaking the ten-show mark Coldplay held.

floor.headline: Elliott's bid and oil thesis deliver; ENPH flatlines
floor.body_md: The macro thesis mostly worked, though with cracks. BIO supplied the day's strongest impulse: Elliott's activist bid proved faster and deeper than anticipated, validating the event-driven overlay on sector rotation. VAL tracked its thesis straightforwardly—with oil up 27.7% in twenty days and stagflation inflating deepwater day rates, the pure-play deepwater operator delivered as expected. ENPH cracked the framework. The technical setup looked clean at entry, but the oil-as-solar-tailwind narrative failed to generate incremental buying. Position flatlined. Macro theses need catalyst confirmation, and extended technicals stall as easily as they resolve. Watch whether the energy bid holds through tomorrow or begins reversing.

floor.headline: Gap exhaustion stalls momentum play on mixed-signal day
floor.body_md:
The momentum rule's worst enemy walked in on day one: contradictory evidence. DOCS.L proved the thesis sound, riding yesterday's 5.79% pop into a +156% gain on clean continuation. ABR told the opposite story. The low-float name that gapped higher yesterday faded through the session, closing below entry, ultimately a -179% loss—the scheduled exit capturing the full drift lower.

Neither outcome was surprising in isolation. Small-cap gaps either sustain into the next session or exhaust overnight demand. The problem: the rule has no mechanism to distinguish between them. Both looked identical at the opening bell.

The basket's 10 trades split 4-5 winners-losers, but ABR's outsized loss outweighed the wins. That's not bad timing or thesis failure—it's the rule working exactly as designed against a regime where its own strength became a liability.

floor.headline: News and sectors lead quieter movers
floor.body_md: News-reactive strategies flourished with triple-digit gains, driven by swings in INTC and peers. Sector rotation maintained steady progress with 32% average returns, while commodity momentum kept pace on USO strength. Mean-reverter proved its reliability, closing 14 positions at a slim 3.88% average—high volume, thin margins the norm for systematic grinders. Bond-cycle faltered with a near-zero loss on IHYG.L. The takeaway: thematic plays—news-reactive, sector-rotator, commodity-momentum—outpaced disciplined mean-reversion on the day, each capturing their slice of modest mid-market gains.
